dataName: sargassum muticum japanese wireweed sargassum algae
name: Japanese Wireweed
description: Invasive brown algae that forms dense floating mats and attaches strongly to hard surfaces like hulls.
features: Brown/yellowish algae
Small air vesicles (look like berries)
Can grow up to several meters long

name: Wakame
description: Marine algae listed as invasive in Europe. Grows rapidly on hulls and port infrastructure.
features: Large brown fronds
Distinct central vein (midrib)
Very wavy and frilly base (sporophyll)

name: Star Ascidian
description: Colonial invertebrate that encrusts on hulls, creating gelatinous mats that smother other species.
features: Colonies form star-shaped patterns
Gelatinous/rubbery texture
Various colors (orange, yellow, green, purple)

name: Striped Barnacle
description: Warm-water crustacean. Causes huge hydrodynamic drag on ships and spreads rapidly.
features: Calcareous cone shape
Vertical purple or brown stripes/lines
Direct primary fouling on metal/paint

dataName: rugulopteryx okamurae asian brown weed algae
name: Asian Brown Weed
description: A highly aggressive brown algae that covers massive areas of the rocky seabed. Transferred via anchors and fishing gear.
features: Yellowish-brown, flat, ribbon-like branches
Forms extremely dense mats
Attaches strongly to hard surfaces and ropes

dataName: asparagopsis armata harpoon weed algae red
name: Harpoon Weed
description: An aggressive red algae officially listed in European legislation. Travels entangled in hull structures and equipment.
features: Pink to dark red color
Barbed, harpoon-like hooks on branches
Fluffy or feathery appearance underwater

name: Club Tunicate
description: A filter-feeding sea squirt that creates heavy fouling on hulls and docks, significantly increasing vessel drag.
features: Club or baseball bat shape with a stalk
Leathery, bumpy outer skin
Brownish to yellowish color

dataName: didemnum vexillum carpet sea squirt tunicate
name: Carpet Sea Squirt
description: A highly invasive colonial tunicate that creates fleshy, mat-like colonies that smother native marine life.
features: Pale orange, cream, or off-white color
Looks like dripping wax or vomit
Leathery, veiny surface

name: Chinese Mitten Crab
description: Invasive crustacean that travels hidden in crevices or in ballast water. Migrates between the sea and freshwater estuaries.
features: Claws covered in dense 'wool' or 'hair'
Olive-green to brown carapace

name: Soft-shell Clam
description: Bivalve mollusc frequently found in sediments that accumulate at the bottom of sea chests of ships stationary for a long time.
features: Oval and fragile (chalky) shell
Long fleshy siphon (tube)

name: Asian Shore Crab
description: A highly invasive crab replacing native species in Northern Europe. Often transported in sea chests and ballast water.
features: Square-shaped carapace
Claws with red/blood-like spots

name: Sea Walnut
description: A devastating comb jelly transported in ballast water. It caused the collapse of fisheries in the Black Sea.
features: Transparent, walnut-shaped body
Lobed structure (no tentacles)
Rows of cilia that diffract light (rainbow effect)

name: Atlantic Blue Crab
description: A voracious predator, originally from the Americas, now devastating bivalve populations in the Mediterranean.
features: Bright blue claws
Olive green carapace with sharp lateral spines

dataName: rapana venosa veined rapa whelk snail
name: Veined Rapa Whelk
description: A large predatory sea snail that feeds on native oysters and mussels. Larvae spread via ballast water.
features: Heavy, rounded shell (up to 18 cm)
Bright orange coloring on the inside of the shell opening

name: Striped Eel Catfish
description: The only marine fish on the EU list of invasive alien species. Highly venomous and dangerous to divers.
features: Eel-like body with four pairs of barbels around the mouth
Brown-black body with two white/yellow horizontal stripes

name: Asian Green Mussel
description: A large, fast-growing mussel that severely fouls hulls, sea chests, and intake pipes throughout the Caribbean, Gulf of Mexico, and South America.
features: Vivid green to dark green outer shell edge
Smooth, elongated, and teardrop-shaped
Can grow up to 16 cm (6 inches) long
Forms extremely dense clusters on hard structures

name: European Green Crab
description: Listed as one of the world's 100 worst invasive species. It travels globally via ballast water and sea chests, devastating native bivalve populations along North and South American coasts.
features: Five distinct spines on the outside edge of each eye
Carapace is broader than it is long
Color ranges from dark green to brown or reddish patches
Highly aggressive when approached

name: Zebra Mussel
description: A freshwater and estuarine bivalve introduced via ballast water. It causes billions of dollars in damage by clogging cooling systems and ship internal piping in North America.
features: Small, D-shaped shell
Distinctive zigzag or striped black-and-white pattern
Sits flat on its underside
Typically very small (the size of a fingernail)

name: Orange Cup Coral
description: An invasive stony coral that heavily fouls ship hulls and oil rigs in the Gulf of Mexico and Brazil, outcompeting native sponges and corals.
features: Bright orange, red, or yellow polyps
Forms cup-like skeletal structures
Does not rely on sunlight (can grow in dark sea chests)
Extends bright yellow tentacles at night to feed

dataName: ciona intestinalis vase tunicate sea squirt
name: Vase Tunicate
description: A solitary sea squirt that creates massive biofouling weight on vessel hulls and aquaculture gear across both coasts of the Americas.
features: Tubular, translucent, jelly-like body
Yellow or green tint at the tips of its siphons
Grows densely packed together like tubes
Can squirt water when squeezed or removed

name: Skeleton Shrimp
description: A highly invasive amphipod that travels in secondary biofouling (living on algae that grows on hulls). Extremely prevalent in US and Canadian ports.
features: Looks like an underwater praying mantis or stick insect
Thread-like body, often red or brown
Clings to algae, ropes, and buoys in massive swarms
Males have large, fuzzy claws

name: Black-striped Mussel
description: A highly destructive marine and brackish bivalve. It forms massive, dense layers on ship hulls, sea water piping, and port infrastructure, often destroying local biodiversity.
features: Small shell (up to 2.5 cm)
Dark brown to black stripes or completely black/dark brown
Very similar to the Zebra Mussel, but lives in marine/estuarine environments
Grows in exceptionally tight, multi-layered clusters

region: americas na-pacific sa-pacific na-atlantic sa-atlantic gulf-mexico caribbean
name: Red Rust Bryozoan
description: A critical biofouling threat because it is highly resistant to copper-based anti-fouling paints. Once it attaches to a hull, it provides a safe surface for other invasive species to settle on.
features: Forms flat, crusty, or leafy colonies
Striking bright red, orange, or rust-black color
Hard, brittle texture like a potato chip
Often found directly on anti-fouling paint

name: Red Lionfish
description: One of the most devastating invasive fish in the Atlantic and Caribbean. While introduced via aquarium trade/ballast, port divers frequently encounter them hiding in sea chests, dock pilings, and wrecks.
features: Distinctive red, maroon, and white vertical stripes
Large, fan-like pectoral fins
Long, separated dorsal spines containing potent venom
Extremely dangerous to handle

name: Australian Tubeworm
description: A reef-building tubeworm that thrives in ports and estuaries. It rapidly encrusts hulls and propellers with heavy, rock-hard calcium carbonate tubes.
features: Builds intertwining, white calcareous (chalky) tubes
Tubes are typically 1-2 mm wide but form massive reefs
Worm has a feathery, brown/green crown when extended
Leaves sharp, razor-like crusts when scraped off

name: Snowflake Coral
description: A highly invasive soft coral that grows rapidly on shaded artificial structures, metal hulls, and oil rigs, completely smothering native black corals.
features: Erect, branching, tree-like stems
Branches are covered in beautiful, white, star-like polyps
Stems are often pale pink or orange beneath the white polyps
Thrives in dark niche areas and under piers

name: Australian Spotted Jellyfish
description: While adults are free-swimming and clog fishing nets, they spread globally because their polyp stage attaches to ship hulls like biofouling.
features: Large, brownish bell covered in bright white spots
Chunky, cauliflower-like oral arms instead of trailing tentacles
Can grow up to 50 cm (20 inches) across
Forms massive swarms in the Gulf of Mexico

name: Brown Bryozoan
description: A notorious and widespread macro-fouling organism. It creates immense hydrodynamic drag on vessels and easily transports across oceans on hull surfaces.
features: Looks like a bushy, dark purple or reddish-brown seaweed
Actually an animal colony, not a plant
Grows in clumps up to 8-10 cm high
Feels slightly stiff or crispy compared to soft algae

name: Killer Algae
description: A highly toxic, aggressive green algae that can clone itself from tiny fragments left on anchors or hull crevices. Eradicated in California but remains a strict watch species.
features: Bright, neon green color
Looks like underwater fern or flattened feathers
Grows rapidly on a horizontal runner (stolon)
Toxic to most marine herbivores

name: Orange-striped Green Anemone
description: A tiny but incredibly resilient anemone that thrives on ship hulls and survives extreme temperature and salinity changes during ocean crossings.
features: Small body, rarely larger than 3 cm
Olive green column usually marked with vertical orange or yellow stripes
Up to 100 thin, translucent tentacles
Often found growing among mussels and barnacles on hulls
